judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 1 correctly identifies the RESULTS_DB collision risk and mitigation (hit d) but structurally defers F-11 to a separate later wave rather than the early concurrent stage (miss b); Report 2 nails the staged ordering with F-11 (hit b) but incorrectly dismisses the RESULTS_DB risk as unnecessary setup (miss d) — both tie on a/c, so overall the reports are comparable, each missing one of the two harder expectations.

report_1.evidence: Eng A — registry.py: F-02, F-05, F-09. Three independent one-liners, one file, one owner (they'd otherwise collide line-by-line if split).

report_2.evidence: Lane A — pipeline/registry.py: F-02, F-05, F-09. Three independent one-line config edits ... Trivial, no coupling. One engineer.

Lane C — pipeline/rubric.py + rubric test: F-01, then F-11. ... Ordering across lanes: A and E are the lowest-risk, no-decision, fast-to-merge lanes — land them first ... B and C run fully in parallel with everyone else (different files).

report_1.evidence: F-06 — behavior change, not a bug. ... I'd batch it for a product/owner decision on the new PARTIAL_CREDIT_THRESHOLDS values before anyone edits score.py.

report_2.evidence: F-06 — not a mechanical fix; it needs a decision. ... That is a scoring-policy call the reviewer/product owner must sign off on, plus a re-baselining of expected scores — not something an engineer should pick numbers for unilaterally.

report_1.evidence: Their own RESULTS_DB, e.g. export RESULTS_DB=/var/tmp/llm-scoring/<name>.db, so parallel test runs don't share the single default DB in tests/conftest.py.

report_2.evidence: Note (not a blocker): tests/conftest.py reads RESULTS_DB (default /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db); nothing in the current tests uses it, so no setup is required, but it's there if new tests need a results DB.
